name: Celecoxib for skin cancer prevention
summary: In a small trial of patients with extensive actinic keratoses, the COX-2 inhibitor celecoxib roughly halved new nonmelanoma skin cancers, including both squamous- and basal-cell carcinomas — but cardiovascular safety concerns limit its use as a preventive.
description: A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of 240 patients with actinic keratoses treated celecoxib 200 mg twice daily for 9 months, with skin cancers counted at 11 months.
interpretation: Celecoxib reduced all three endpoints (nonmelanoma skin cancer overall, squamous- and basal-cell carcinoma) by roughly half in a high-risk population, so the NNTs are low. The major caveat is not efficacy but safety: COX-2 inhibitors carry cardiovascular risk, and this was a small, short trial — so celecoxib is not used routinely for skin cancer prevention. See the glossary for rate ratio and NNT.
methodologyNotes: Effects are the published tumor rate ratios with 95% CIs; the control-arm lesion rates (0.35 nonmelanoma, 0.19 squamous-cell, 0.16 basal-cell tumors per patient) are used as the baseline so NNT reflects high-risk patients treated per cancer prevented over the ~11-month trial. Single, small trial (n=240).

studies.author: Elmets
studies.citation: Elmets CA, Viner JL, Pentland AP, et al. Chemoprevention of nonmelanoma skin cancer with celecoxib: a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. J Natl Cancer Inst. 2010;102:1835-1844.
